facts.text: The word 'ski' comes from the Old Norse word 'skíð,' meaning a split piece of wood or firewood.
Ancient rock carvings in Norway dating to around 4000 BCE depict figures on skis, making skiing one of the oldest forms of transport.
Chamonix, France hosted the first Winter Olympic Games in 1924, which included cross-country skiing, ski jumping, and Nordic combined.
Alpine skiing was not included in the Olympics until the 1936 Winter Games in Garmisch-Partenkirchen, Germany.
Ingemar Stenmark of Sweden won 86 World Cup races, a record that stood for decades as the most in alpine skiing history.
Modern ski bindings are designed to release during a fall, significantly reducing the risk of leg and knee injuries.
The Vasaloppet in Sweden is the oldest and longest cross-country ski race in the world, covering 90 kilometers from Sälen to Mora.
Aspen, Colorado became a popular ski destination after hosting the 1950 FIS Alpine World Ski Championships.
Avalanche transceivers, also known as beacons, transmit a signal that allows buried skiers to be located by rescue teams.
Warren Miller produced over 60 ski and snowboard films from 1950 to 2004, becoming legendary in ski culture.
Lindsey Vonn won 82 World Cup races during her career, making her the most successful female alpine ski racer in history.
The Four Hills Tournament in ski jumping takes place annually across four venues in Germany and Austria.
Mikaela Shiffrin broke Ingemar Stenmark's all-time record of 86 World Cup victories in March 2023.
Ski wax reduces friction between the ski base and snow, and different wax types are used for different snow temperatures.
St. Moritz in Switzerland hosted the Winter Olympics twice, in 1928 and 1948.
Cross-country skiing uses two main techniques: the classic stride, which follows set tracks, and the skate technique, which resembles ice skating.
Eddie 'The Eagle' Edwards became a folk hero at the 1988 Calgary Olympics despite finishing last in ski jumping.
Heli-skiing involves being dropped by helicopter onto remote, ungroomed mountain terrain for fresh powder runs.
The giant slalom was introduced to the Winter Olympics in 1952 at the Oslo Games.
Whistler Blackcomb in British Columbia has the longest vertical drop of any ski resort in North America at 1,609 meters.
Alberto Tomba of Italy was nicknamed 'La Bomba' and won three Olympic gold medals in slalom and giant slalom.
Ski poles were originally single long poles used for balance and braking before evolving into the paired poles used today.
The first mechanical ski lift, a J-bar, was installed in Davos, Switzerland in 1934.
Freestyle skiing debuted as an Olympic discipline at the 1992 Albertville Games with the moguls event.
Avalanche airbag packs inflate to help keep a skier on top of sliding snow during an avalanche.
Jean-Claude Killy of France swept all three alpine skiing events at the 1968 Grenoble Olympics.
Niseko in Hokkaido, Japan is famous for receiving some of the driest, lightest powder snow in the world.
The super-G event combines the speed of downhill with the technical turns of giant slalom.
Bode Miller is the only American male alpine skier to win five overall World Cup discipline titles.
Après-ski is the French term for socializing and relaxing after a day of skiing, often involving food and drinks at lodges.
The Birkebeiner race in Norway commemorates a 1206 event where two warrior scouts carried the infant King Haakon IV to safety on skis.
Zermatt, Switzerland offers skiing with views of the iconic Matterhorn and has slopes that stay open nearly year-round.
The first downhill ski race was held in 1911 at Montana, Switzerland, organized by Sir Arnold Lunn.
Ski goggles protect against UV radiation, wind, and snow glare, and use different lens tints for varying light conditions.
Hermann Maier of Austria was nicknamed 'The Herminator' for his aggressive and powerful skiing style.
Park City, Utah is home to the United States Ski and Snowboard Association headquarters.
Cat skiing uses snowcats to transport skiers to backcountry terrain, offering a less expensive alternative to heli-skiing.
The slalom event requires skiers to navigate through a series of closely spaced gates, demanding precise turns.
Marcel Hirscher of Austria won the overall World Cup title eight consecutive times from 2012 to 2019.
The Hahnenkamm downhill race in Kitzbühel, Austria is considered one of the most dangerous races on the World Cup circuit.
Skis were originally made from a single piece of wood, often ash or hickory, before transitioning to laminated and composite materials.
The biathlon combines cross-country skiing with rifle marksmanship and has been an Olympic sport since 1960.
Verbier in Switzerland is renowned for its challenging off-piste terrain and vibrant après-ski scene.
Picabo Street won Olympic gold in the super-G at the 1998 Nagano Games.
The FIS Alpine Ski World Cup was established in 1967 and runs annually from October to March.
Mogul skiing involves navigating a field of bumps while performing aerial tricks, judged on speed, turns, and air.
Jackson Hole Mountain Resort in Wyoming is famous for its expert terrain, including the iconic Corbet's Couloir.
The first Winter Olympics held in Asia took place in Sapporo, Japan in 1972.
Ski helmets became widely adopted in the 2000s after several high-profile skiing accidents.
The Lauberhorn downhill in Wengen, Switzerland is the longest downhill race on the World Cup circuit at 4.48 kilometers.
Vail, Colorado opened in 1962 and became one of the largest ski resorts in North America.
The telemark turn, developed in the Telemark region of Norway, involves a lunging motion with one ski forward.
Slopestyle skiing was added to the Winter Olympics at the 2014 Sochi Games.
Cross-country skiers have among the highest measured VO2 max values of any athletes in the world.
The Arlberg region of Austria is often considered the birthplace of modern alpine skiing instruction.
Speed skiing holds the record as the fastest non-motorized sport on land, with speeds exceeding 250 km/h.
Lindsey Vonn won the Olympic downhill gold medal at the 2010 Vancouver Games.
Ski boots have evolved from leather lace-up designs to rigid plastic shells with precise buckle systems.
The first ski resort in the United States was Sun Valley, Idaho, which opened in 1936.
Chamonix-Mont-Blanc is home to the Vallée Blanche, one of the most famous off-piste ski runs in the world.
Snowplowing, also known as the pizza or wedge technique, is the first braking method taught to beginner skiers.
The combined alpine event features one downhill run and one slalom run, with times added together.
Aspen has four distinct ski areas: Aspen Mountain, Aspen Highlands, Buttermilk, and Snowmass.
Italy's Dolomites region features dramatic limestone peaks and world-class ski resorts like Cortina d'Ampezzo.
The 1994 Lillehammer Winter Olympics are often considered the greatest Winter Games ever held.
Parabolic or shaped skis, introduced in the 1990s, have a wider tip and tail that make turning easier.
Matti Nykänen of Finland is considered one of the greatest ski jumpers of all time, winning four Olympic gold medals.
The K-point in ski jumping marks the distance where the landing hill begins to flatten, used as a benchmark score.
Backcountry skiing requires carrying safety equipment including a shovel, probe, and avalanche beacon.
Vail's Back Bowls offer over 3,000 acres of wide-open terrain for intermediate and advanced skiers.
The FIS Nordic World Ski Championships are held in odd-numbered years, alternating with the Winter Olympics.
Toni Sailer of Austria became the first skier to win all three alpine events at a single Olympics in 1956.
Skiing in Chile and Argentina allows enthusiasts to ski during the Northern Hemisphere's summer months.
The Ski Club of Great Britain, founded in 1903, is one of the oldest ski clubs in the world.
Snow cannons, or snowmaking machines, were first used commercially in the 1950s to supplement natural snowfall.
Mikaela Shiffrin won her first World Cup slalom race at age 17, becoming the youngest slalom champion in World Cup history.
The Vallée Blanche in Chamonix is a 20-kilometer off-piste route that descends through glacial terrain.
Ski cross, a freestyle skiing event where four to six skiers race head-to-head on a course, debuted at the 2010 Olympics.
Whistler hosted alpine skiing events during the 2010 Vancouver Winter Olympics.
The Kandahar ski club, founded by Sir Arnold Lunn, pioneered competitive downhill and slalom racing.
Modern slalom gates are made of hinged plastic poles that flex when contacted, replacing rigid bamboo poles.
Bode Miller won Olympic gold in the super combined at the 2010 Vancouver Games.
The Engadin Skimarathon in Switzerland is one of the world's largest cross-country ski races with over 13,000 participants.
Ski mountaineering, or skimo, combines skiing with mountaineering and became an Olympic sport for 2026.
Cortina d'Ampezzo in Italy hosted the Winter Olympics in 1956 and is set to co-host again in 2026.
The first chairlift was installed at Sun Valley, Idaho in 1936, adapted from a system used to load bananas onto ships.
GS (giant slalom) gates are spaced farther apart than slalom gates, allowing for higher speeds and wider turns.
Hermann Maier survived a spectacular crash at the 1998 Nagano Olympics downhill but came back to win two gold medals.
The Swedish Vasaloppet race takes place on the first Sunday of March each year and attracts over 15,000 participants.
Powder skiing involves skiing on deep, freshly fallen snow, which many skiers consider the ultimate experience.
The Wengen World Cup races have been held continuously since 1930, making them the oldest on the circuit.
Halfpipe skiing was added to the Winter Olympics at the 2014 Sochi Games.
The Arlberg technique, developed by Hannes Schneider in the 1920s, became the foundation of modern ski instruction.
St. Anton am Arlberg in Austria is considered one of the birthplaces of alpine skiing.
A ski's sidecut radius determines the tightness of turns it can carve—smaller radius means tighter turns.
The Winter Olympics were held in the same year as the Summer Olympics until 1992, after which they were staggered.
Kjetil André Aamodt of Norway won the most Olympic medals in alpine skiing with eight total.
The Streif in Kitzbühel has a maximum gradient of 85%, making it one of the steepest race courses in the world.
Skis for cross-country skiing are narrower and lighter than alpine skis, designed for efficient forward gliding.
The 2018 PyeongChang Winter Olympics featured 11 alpine skiing events.
Grooming machines, or snowcats, flatten and condition ski slopes to create smooth corduroy surfaces.
Ingemar Stenmark won two Olympic gold medals in slalom and giant slalom at the 1980 Lake Placid Games.
The longest ski run in the world is the Parsenn run from Weissfluhjoch to Küblis in Switzerland, stretching about 12 kilometers.
Aerial skiing involves launching off jumps and performing acrobatic maneuvers, first included in the Olympics in 1994.
The concept of fall-line skiing means pointing your skis directly down the steepest gradient of the slope.
Alpine skiing's World Cup season traditionally begins in late October at the glacier above Sölden, Austria.
Val d'Isère in France co-hosts the annual Criterium de la Première Neige, one of the earliest World Cup races each season.
Ski touring involves ascending slopes on skis fitted with climbing skins and then skiing back down.
The 1976 Winter Olympics in Innsbruck, Austria saw Franz Klammer's legendary gold medal downhill run.
Shaped skis revolutionized the sport in the mid-1990s by making carved turns accessible to recreational skiers.
The maximum speed ever recorded in speed skiing is 254.958 km/h, set by Ivan Origone in 2016.
Killington, Vermont has hosted women's World Cup slalom and giant slalom races since 2016.
The Christie turn, named after the Norwegian city Christiania (now Oslo), is a parallel turning technique.
Ski bindings have a DIN setting that determines the force required for them to release during a fall.
The FIS (Fédération Internationale de Ski) was founded on February 2, 1924, in Chamonix.
Norway has won more Winter Olympic medals than any other country, with cross-country skiing being their strongest event.
The 'ski bum' lifestyle emerged in the 1960s and 70s as young people moved to ski towns to ski as much as possible.
Squaw Valley (now Palisades Tahoe) hosted the 1960 Winter Olympics and introduced computerized race timing.
Carving involves tilting the ski on its edge so the sidecut shape naturally guides the ski through a turn.
The term 'yard sale' in skiing refers to a crash where equipment is scattered across the slope.
Whistler Blackcomb receives an average of over 11 meters of snowfall annually.
Mikaela Shiffrin has won World Cup races in all six alpine disciplines: slalom, GS, super-G, downhill, combined, and parallel.
The Andes in South America have numerous ski resorts, with Portillo in Chile being one of the most famous.
Snowboarding's addition to the Olympics in 1998 initially created tension with traditional skiing communities.
The first gondola lift in the world was installed at Badgastein, Austria in 1949.
Terrain parks with jumps, rails, and boxes originated from snowboarding culture but are now popular with freestyle skiers.
The Lauberhorn race in Wengen features a section called the Hundschopf, a dramatic cliff jump.
Skiing generates an estimated $20 billion annually in economic activity in the United States alone.
The first patent for an artificial snow machine was awarded in 1961.
Telemark skiing, which uses a free-heel binding, is named after the Telemark region of Norway where it originated.
The 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics featured alpine skiing events at the Yanqing National Alpine Ski Center.
Twin-tip skis, designed for skiing backwards (switch), became popular with the rise of freestyle skiing in the 2000s.
Ski resorts use RFID-enabled lift passes that allow skiers to go through gates without removing the pass from a pocket.
The Eiger Ultra Trail passes through Wengen, connecting skiing and trail running communities in the Swiss Alps.
Alberto Tomba won his first Olympic gold medal in the giant slalom at the 1988 Calgary Games.
An avalanche can reach speeds of 130 km/h within five seconds of starting.
The Parallel Giant Slalom was briefly included in the Olympics as a team event at the 2018 PyeongChang Games.
Scandinavia's friluftsliv philosophy—embracing outdoor life—heavily influenced skiing's cultural significance.
The term 'schuss' means to ski straight downhill without turning, from the German word for 'shot.'
Lake Louise in Alberta, Canada is a regular stop on the women's World Cup circuit for downhill and super-G events.
Ski wax is categorized into glide wax for speed and grip wax for traction in cross-country skiing.
The Dolomiti Superski pass covers 12 ski areas and over 1,200 kilometers of slopes in northeastern Italy.
Gretchen Fraser became the first American to win an Olympic gold medal in alpine skiing at the 1948 St. Moritz Games.
Night skiing, using floodlights on slopes, first became popular in the 1930s and 40s.
The Sno-Cat, invented in the 1940s, became essential for grooming ski slopes and accessing backcountry terrain.
Picabo Street was named after the tiny town of Picabo, Idaho, near where she grew up.
The snow line—the elevation above which snow remains year-round—is rising due to climate change, affecting ski resorts worldwide.
Kitzbühel's Hahnenkamm race weekend is one of the most-watched annual sporting events in Europe.
The first women's alpine skiing events at the Olympics were held at the 1936 Garmisch-Partenkirchen Games.
A 'bluebird day' in skiing refers to a day with clear blue skies and fresh snow—considered perfect conditions.
The Norwegian military used ski troops as early as the 18th century, establishing formal ski warfare training.
Steamboat Springs, Colorado is known as 'Ski Town, USA' and has produced more Winter Olympians than any other town in North America.
The super-G was added to the Winter Olympics in 1988 at the Calgary Games.
Adaptive skiing allows people with physical disabilities to ski using specialized equipment like sit-skis and outriggers.
The Trois Vallées (Three Valleys) in France is the largest linked ski area in the world, covering 600 kilometers of slopes.
Kamil Stoch of Poland is one of only two ski jumpers to win all four events of the Four Hills Tournament in a single year.
Jean-Claude Killy retired from racing at age 24 after his triple gold medal performance at the 1968 Olympics.
The first ski school in North America was established at Sugar Hill, New Hampshire in 1929.
Snowmaking requires temperatures below about −2°C (28°F) to produce snow that will stick on the ground.
The Ganslern slalom course in Kitzbühel has a steep pitch of up to 63% and icy conditions that challenge the world's best.
Mammoth Mountain in California once recorded 2,407 centimeters (78.9 feet) of snow in a single season in 2010-2011.
The first Winter X Games were held in 1997 and helped popularize freestyle skiing events.
Skiing on volcanic slopes is possible in places like Mount Etna in Sicily and Mount Ruapehu in New Zealand.
The National Ski Patrol was founded in 1938 by Charles Minot Dole to provide safety services at ski areas.
Snowploughs and stem christies are intermediate turning techniques between wedge turns and full parallel skiing.
Courchevel in France hosted skiing events during the 2023 FIS Alpine World Ski Championships.
The FIS sets course specifications for all World Cup and Olympic alpine skiing events, including gate spacing and vertical drop.
Swiss ski champion Bernhard Russi designed several Olympic downhill courses, including the one used at the 2006 Turin Games.
Cross-country ski races range from sprint events of about 1.5 km to the grueling 50 km mass start.
The ski industry employs over 200,000 people in the United States during the winter season.
A 'corn snow' surface forms in spring when snow melts during the day and refreezes at night, creating a granular texture.
The 10th Mountain Division, a US Army unit trained for mountain and winter warfare, helped develop several Colorado ski resorts after World War II.
Alta, Utah is one of the few remaining ski resorts in North America that does not allow snowboarding.
The longest ski jump ever recorded is 253.5 meters, set by Stefan Kraft of Austria in 2017.
Ski patrollers undergo rigorous training in first aid, avalanche safety, and rescue techniques.
Jackson Hole's famous Corbet's Couloir requires skiers to drop into a narrow, steep chute from a cornice.
The Audi FIS Ski World Cup awards a large crystal globe to the overall season champion.
Heated ski boots with battery-powered warming elements have become increasingly popular for cold-weather skiing.
The Olympic downhill course must have a minimum vertical drop of 800 meters for men and 450 meters for women.
La Grave in France is famous for having no groomed runs—skiers navigate the mountain entirely off-piste.
The first artificial ski slope in the world opened in London in 1965, using a plastic bristle surface.
Parallel skiing, where both skis remain parallel throughout turns, is considered the standard technique for intermediate and advanced skiers.
Innsbruck, Austria has hosted the Winter Olympics twice, in 1964 and 1976.
The detachable chairlift, which slows down at loading and unloading points, was introduced in the 1980s.
Ski racing categories for children are based on age and progress from Microstar through J1 classifications.
Lech and Zürs in Austria form part of the Ski Arlberg region, one of the largest interconnected ski areas in Austria.
Cross-country skiing burns more calories per hour than almost any other sport, averaging 500-1,000 calories per hour.
The Burton Open, while a snowboard event, helped elevate halfpipe culture that influenced freestyle skiing.
A ski resort's vertical drop is the difference in elevation between the summit and the base, and is a key measure of a resort's size.
Lindsey Vonn announced her retirement from competitive skiing on February 10, 2019.
Ski jumping hills are classified as normal (HS 85-109) and large (HS 110+) based on the hill size.
The Friends of the Indy Pass coalition promotes smaller, independent ski hills across North America.
Helmet use among skiers in the US increased from about 25% in 2003 to over 80% by 2020.
Glenshee in Scotland is Scotland's largest ski area, though its season is often limited by unpredictable weather.
Tuckerman Ravine on Mount Washington in New Hampshire is a legendary backcountry ski destination in the eastern US.
The European Ski AMADE pass covers 760 km of slopes and 270 lifts across the Salzburg region of Austria.
The Ikon Pass and Epic Pass are multi-resort season passes that have transformed how skiers access different mountains.
Ski mountaineering races include both ascending and descending sections, testing uphill fitness and downhill skill.
The first rope tow for skiing was installed in Shawbridge, Quebec, Canada in 1932.
In ski jumping, style judges evaluate body position, ski placement, and landing stability, awarding up to 20 points each.
Cervinia in Italy and Zermatt in Switzerland share a connected ski area that straddles the Italian-Swiss border.
Snow density varies widely—fresh powder can be as light as 5% water content, while wet spring snow reaches 50%.
The Winter Olympics in Calgary in 1988 were the first to feature a combined event that used separate downhill and slalom runs.
Marcel Hirscher won every overall World Cup title from the 2011-12 season through 2018-19, an unprecedented streak of eight.
Portillo, Chile was the site of the first World Ski Championships held in the Southern Hemisphere in 1966.
The tip, waist, and tail dimensions of a ski define its shape and determine its performance characteristics.
Tignes in France features a glacier that allows skiing even in summer months.
The American Birkebeiner is the largest cross-country ski race in North America, held annually in Hayward, Wisconsin.
Bode Miller became the first American man to win the overall World Cup title in 2005.
The PSIA (Professional Ski Instructors of America) sets standards for ski teaching across the United States.
Ski racing gates are color-coded: red and blue alternate in slalom, while GS uses red and blue on opposite sides.
The first enclosed gondola in North America was installed at Wildcat Mountain, New Hampshire in 1957.
Gravity-fed snowmaking uses the natural pressure from elevated water sources to power snow guns without pumps.
Val Thorens in France is the highest ski resort in Europe at 2,300 meters, guaranteeing reliable snow conditions.
The World Cup combined event was reformatted in 2007 to feature a single shorter downhill run alongside the slalom.
Ski films like 'Hot Dog...The Movie' (1984) helped popularize freestyle skiing in popular culture.
The rocker or reverse camber design in skis lifts the tip and tail, improving flotation in deep powder.
Mayrhofen in Austria hosts the Harakiri piste, the steepest groomed run in the country at a 78% gradient.
Norway's Marit Bjørgen is the most decorated Winter Olympian of all time, with 15 medals in cross-country skiing.
The FIS introduced a parallel slalom format for the World Cup, featuring head-to-head elimination racing.
Snow fences are used at ski resorts and along mountain roads to control wind-blown snow accumulation.
Levi in Finnish Lapland hosts the first slalom races of the World Cup season each November.
Ski touring bindings have a walk mode that allows the heel to lift freely for climbing and lock down for descending.
The Five-Ring Circus is a nickname for the competitive spectacle of Olympic alpine skiing.
In 1924, the first Olympic cross-country skiing event was the 18 km race for men.
Skiing at night under a full moon, known as moonlight skiing, is offered at select resorts as a unique experience.
The Arosa Lenzerheide ski area in Switzerland hosts the season-ending World Cup finals regularly.
Skiing's popularity surged after World War II when returning soldiers brought their newly learned mountain skills home.
Kilian Jornet, known for ultrarunning, is also an accomplished ski mountaineer who has set speed records on major peaks.
The K2 brand, named after the second-tallest mountain in the world, has been making skis since 1962.
NASTAR (National Standard Race) allows recreational skiers in the US to compare their times against a national standard.
The world's highest ski resort by base elevation is Chacaltaya in Bolivia, though it lost its glacier and closed.
Snowcat skiing operations in British Columbia include companies like Island Lake Lodge and Great Canadian Heli-Skiing.
Race suits for alpine skiing are tight-fitting to reduce wind resistance and have padding at key impact points.
The FIS established the Super-G World Cup in the 1982-83 season.
Gulmarg in Kashmir, India has one of the highest gondolas in the world, reaching 3,979 meters.
The Inferno race in Mürren, Switzerland is the oldest and longest amateur downhill ski race in the world.
Solar-powered ski lifts have been installed at several resorts as part of sustainability initiatives.
A 'white-out' occurs when flat light and snow make it nearly impossible to see terrain features while skiing.
The Norwegian poet and author Fridtjof Nansen crossed Greenland on skis in 1888, inspiring wider interest in skiing.
Ski jumping's V-style technique, where skis form a V rather than staying parallel, was pioneered by Jan Boklöv in the 1980s.
Many ski resorts now offer summer activities including mountain biking, zip-lining, and alpine coasters.
The first cable car used for skiing was built in Bolzano, Italy in 1908, though it was originally designed for tourists.
Kirkwood Mountain Resort in California receives some of the deepest snow in the Lake Tahoe region.
The FIS requires that alpine race skis meet minimum length and radius specifications to ensure safety.
Powder skis are wider (100mm+ at the waist) to provide flotation in deep snow.
The Winter Olympics in Sarajevo in 1984 were notable for the warm hospitality of the host city.
Freestyle skiing's slopestyle event combines jumps, rails, and other features in a creative ski park course.
Pre-release, when a binding opens during normal skiing rather than a crash, is a dangerous equipment malfunction.
Kicking Horse Mountain Resort in British Columbia is known for its long vertical drop and challenging inbounds terrain.
The tradition of torch-lit ski descents on New Year's Eve is practiced at many European and North American resorts.
Eddie 'The Eagle' Edwards' story was made into a 2016 film starring Taron Egerton and Hugh Jackman.
Ski areas measure snowfall using snow stakes and manual snow boards positioned at representative elevations.
The European Avalanche Warning Services uses a five-level danger scale from 'Low' to 'Very High.'
Hannes Schneider, an Austrian ski pioneer, developed the Arlberg technique and opened one of the first ski schools in the 1920s.
The Hintertux Glacier in Austria is open for skiing 365 days a year.
Italy's Sellaronda is a ski circuit around the Sella Group in the Dolomites, covering about 40 km with 4 mountain passes.
An edging angle of 45-65 degrees is typical for expert carving turns on groomed slopes.
The Silver Star ski resort in British Columbia is known for consistent dry powder and a charming base village.
The 2026 Winter Olympics will feature skiing events in both Cortina d'Ampezzo and Bormio, Italy.
Recreational skiers typically make between 10 and 30 runs per day, depending on the mountain's vertical drop.
The concept of 'first tracks'—skiing on freshly groomed or untracked snow first thing in the morning—is a prized experience.
Lindsey Vonn's career was marked by multiple injuries and comebacks, including a major knee injury in 2013.
The largest single ski resort by skiable acreage in North America is Big Sky, Montana.
Wanaka and Queenstown in New Zealand offer skiing during the Southern Hemisphere's winter from June to October.
Ski boots are rated by flex index—lower flex for beginners (60-80) and higher flex for advanced skiers (120-150).
The Freeride World Tour features professional skiers competing on ungroomed, natural mountain terrain.
Klammer's winning downhill run at the 1976 Innsbruck Olympics is considered one of the greatest runs in skiing history.
Hochfügen in Austria introduced the first six-seater chairlift in Europe.
The word 'slalom' comes from the Norwegian dialect words 'sla' (slope) and 'låm' (track).
Japan's ski resorts benefit from 'Japow'—the extraordinarily light, dry powder snow that falls in abundance.
Ski simulators and indoor ski slopes, such as those in Dubai and the Netherlands, allow year-round skiing practice.
The Women's World Cup slalom in Flachau, Austria is known as the 'Night Race' and draws huge crowds.
Risk of ACL injuries is higher in skiing than in most other sports, leading to ongoing improvements in binding design.
The FIS Cross-Country World Cup includes stages for individual sprint, team sprint, skiathlon, and distance events.
Snowbird, Utah averages over 500 inches (12.7 meters) of snowfall annually.
The first timed downhill ski race was organized by Sir Arnold Lunn in 1911 at Crans-Montana, Switzerland.
Ski poles are made from aluminum, carbon fiber, or composite materials, with carbon fiber being the lightest.
Revelstoke Mountain Resort in British Columbia has the greatest vertical drop of any ski resort in North America.
The term 'bombing' in skiing slang means skiing very fast straight down the hill.
Climate change is projected to reduce snowfall at lower-elevation resorts by 30-70% by 2100.
The Holmenkollen ski festival in Oslo, Norway has been an annual event since 1892.
GoPro cameras became ubiquitous in skiing, allowing skiers to film their runs from a first-person perspective.
The first women's downhill race at the Olympics was held at the 1948 St. Moritz Games.
Avalanche dogs are trained to locate buried skiers using their sense of smell, and can search a large area faster than probe teams.
The base layer of a ski is made of polyethylene (P-Tex), which absorbs wax to improve glide.
Megève in France was one of the first purpose-built ski resorts, developed by the Rothschild family in the 1910s.
The Euro-Carve, an extreme carving technique where the skier's body nearly touches the snow surface, was popularized in the 2010s.
Ski resort trail maps are color-coded: green circles for beginners, blue squares for intermediate, and black diamonds for expert runs.
The '10th Mountain Division Hut Association' maintains a system of backcountry huts in Colorado for ski touring.
Fat skis with waists wider than 110mm transformed powder skiing by distributing weight over a larger surface area.
The Stelvio Pass in Bormio, Italy hosts one of the most technically demanding World Cup downhill courses.
Thermals and base layers made of merino wool became popular among skiers for their moisture-wicking and temperature-regulating properties.
The FIS Freestyle Ski World Cup awards small crystal globes for each discipline and a large globe for the overall champion.
A 'pizza' (wedge) and 'french fries' (parallel) are the slang terms instructors use to teach children how to stop and glide.
Ski areas in the Southern Alps of New Zealand, such as Treble Cone and Cardrona, are popular with international visitors.
The first ski patrol was organized in 1938 at Stowe, Vermont by Charles Minot Dole.
Goggle lenses with photochromic technology automatically adjust tint based on light conditions.
The Olympic mixed team event in alpine skiing was introduced at the 2018 PyeongChang Games.
Skiing generated approximately $4.2 billion in lift ticket revenue in North America during the 2018-19 season.
The first women's Olympic ski jumping event was held at the 2014 Sochi Games on the normal hill.
Many ski resorts are investing in renewable energy, including wind and solar power, to reduce their carbon footprint.
Skier days—a measure of total visits—in the United States typically range from 50 to 60 million per season.
The alpine combined event at the Olympics was revamped multiple times, alternating between traditional and super combined formats.
Mont Tremblant in Quebec is Eastern Canada's premier ski destination, with the longest vertical drop in Eastern North America.
The 'yard sale' crash is so named because equipment is strewn about as if displayed for sale.
The FIS sets minimum snow depth requirements for race courses to ensure athlete safety.
Italian ski culture includes the tradition of a leisurely multi-course lunch on the mountain, often lasting well over an hour.
The Taos Ski Valley in New Mexico was founded by Ernie Blake, a Swiss-German immigrant, in 1955.
Polymers in modern ski bases are more durable and better at absorbing wax than early designs.
The role of the forerunner in ski racing is to test the course before competitors, without posting an official time.
Snowbird's 'Ski Free' January promotion has been a tradition since the resort's early days.
Ski jump takeoff platforms are angled between 7 and 12 degrees, with the exact angle depending on the hill's design.
Season pass sales have become a major revenue driver for large resort operators like Vail Resorts and Alterra Mountain Company.
Banked slalom, where turns are made on banked snow walls, is a fun event popular at many resorts and festivals.
The Deer Valley ski resort in Park City, Utah limits daily ticket sales to prevent overcrowding on slopes.
Children can typically begin learning to ski at age 3 or 4, starting on gentle slopes with small, lightweight equipment.
The Nordic combined event pairs cross-country skiing with ski jumping and has been in every Winter Olympics since 1924.
Chamonix's Aiguille du Midi cable car reaches 3,842 meters and provides access to legendary off-piste terrain.
The 'Method' is a freestyle skiing grab where the skier reaches behind to grab the ski while arching the back.
Ski towns like Breckenridge, Telluride, and Crested Butte in Colorado were originally 19th-century mining communities.
A snow profile, dug in the backcountry, helps skiers assess snowpack stability and avalanche risk.
The Parallel Turn was considered the gold standard of skiing technique for most of the 20th century.
Magnetic ski racks for vehicles were introduced to make transporting skis safer and more convenient.
Olympic ski racing courses are inspected and approved by a jury that includes the technical delegate, chief of race, and referee.
The 2002 Salt Lake City Winter Olympics were held across multiple Utah ski venues.
Wind hold delays at ski areas occur when high winds make it unsafe to operate chairlifts.
Dynastar, Rossignol, Atomic, Head, Völkl, and Fischer are among the most prominent ski manufacturers.
The 'iron cross' is a freestyle ski trick where the skier crosses their skis in an X shape while airborne.
Ski joring involves being pulled on skis behind a horse, dog, or motor vehicle, and originated in Scandinavia.
Sun Valley, Idaho pioneered the concept of a destination ski resort with luxury accommodations.
The average ski season in the Alps lasts from December through April, though glacier skiing extends the season.
Couloir skiing involves descending steep, narrow gullies in the mountains, a hallmark of extreme skiing.
Hermann Maier came back from a near-fatal motorcycle accident in 2001 to win more World Cup races.
The Stein Eriksen Lodge in Park City is named after the Norwegian Olympic gold medalist who helped popularize skiing in Utah.
Many alpine countries have military ski patrol units that also assist in civilian rescue operations.
Ski resorts in the Rocky Mountains generally receive drier, lighter snow compared to the wetter snow of the Pacific Northwest.
The World Ski Awards, established in 2013, annually recognize the best ski resorts and hotels in the world.
Quick-adjust poles have a locking mechanism that allows skiers to change pole length for uphill touring and downhill skiing.
The evolution of ski technology in the 2000s focused on weight reduction, with some touring skis weighing under 1 kg per ski.
Les Deux Alpes in France features one of the largest glaciers in Europe accessible by ski lift.
The skiing term 'face shot' refers to skiing through such deep powder that snow sprays into the skier's face.
Ski clubs provide community, racing opportunities, and group trips for skiers of all ages and abilities.
The first-ever Winter Youth Olympic Games featuring skiing events were held in Innsbruck in 2012.
Edge tuning involves sharpening and beveling ski edges to optimize grip on hard snow and ice.
Mikaela Shiffrin won her first Olympic gold medal in slalom at the 2014 Sochi Games at age 18.
The ski lift at Alyeska Resort in Alaska offers views of Turnagain Arm and surrounding glaciers.
Jansport originally made backpacks for ski mountaineering before becoming a mainstream backpack brand.
The average downhill World Cup racer reaches speeds of 130 km/h, with peaks above 150 km/h on fast courses.
Ski ballet, also known as acroski, was a demonstration sport at the 1988 and 1992 Olympics before being dropped.
The concept of 'earnable turns'—hiking for your skiing—is central to backcountry and ski touring culture.
Mount Hutt in New Zealand is the closest major ski field to Christchurch, opening as early as June each year.
The National Brotherhood of Skiers promotes diversity in snow sports and hosts an annual summit for Black skiers.
Whitefish Mountain Resort in Montana offers affordable lift tickets and over 3,000 acres of skiable terrain.
Ski bindings were first standardized in the 1960s with the introduction of the DIN (Deutsches Institut für Normung) release standard.
Björn Dæhlie of Norway won 12 Olympic medals in cross-country skiing, including 8 golds.
The Haute Route from Chamonix to Zermatt is one of the most famous ski touring routes in the world, taking about a week to complete.
The 1980 Lake Placid Winter Olympics were the last Games to feature the 'classic' alpine combined format.
Smugglers' Notch in Vermont is consistently rated one of the best family ski resorts in the eastern United States.
Ski areas in Scotland include Cairngorm, Nevis Range, Glencoe, Glenshee, and The Lecht.
The 'safety bar' on chairlifts was introduced to prevent skiers from falling during the ride.
Snowcat skiing provides access to untracked powder without the expense of a helicopter.
Gravity-defying cliff drops and spine skiing define the extreme side of the Freeride World Tour.
The term 'gaper' in ski culture refers to an inexperienced skier, often identified by a gap between goggles and helmet.
Ski resorts in Japan serve ramen noodles and curry rice as popular on-mountain lunch options.
The Aiguille Rouge in Les Arcs, France offers a continuous 2,226-meter vertical descent.
The Sochi 2014 Olympics introduced ski halfpipe and slopestyle to the official program for the first time.
Vibram soles on ski boots provide better grip on icy surfaces when walking to and from the slopes.
The race bib, worn over a skier's suit, displays their start number and sponsors for TV visibility.
Lindsey Vonn won 20 World Cup crystal globe titles across downhill, super-G, combined, and overall categories.
Indoor ski halls like SnowWorld in the Netherlands have slopes up to 520 meters long with real snow.
Ski mountaineering will make its Olympic debut at the 2026 Milan-Cortina Games.
The tuck position used in downhill skiing minimizes air resistance and can increase speed by 10-15%.
Jonny Moseley's groundbreaking 'Dinner Roll' trick at the 2002 Olympics popularized creative freestyle skiing.
The Stowe Mountain Resort in Vermont has been called the 'Ski Capital of the East.'
The Davo Karničar became the first person to ski from the summit of Mount Everest in 2000.
Side-country skiing refers to easily accessible off-piste terrain just outside resort boundaries.
The Zermatt-Cervinia ski area became the highest linked ski area between two countries with the Klein Matterhorn connection.
Piste maps are designed to flatten the mountain perspective, giving an overview of all runs and lifts.
The Lahti Ski Games in Finland is one of the oldest and most prestigious Nordic skiing events.
Heated chairlifts with bubble covers have been installed at luxury resorts to improve rider comfort.
The practice of ski tuning includes base grinding, edge sharpening, and waxing for optimal performance.
Andorra's Grandvalira is the largest ski area in the Pyrenees, spanning 210 kilometers of slopes.
Race timing in alpine skiing uses photoelectric cells at the start and finish, accurate to one hundredth of a second.
